Entries.Name: Remote Commands (DCOM)
Entries.Description: Control DCOM and remote command surfaces
Entries.Detail: Controls DCOM remote activation and device metadata file associations. DCOM is required by SCCM, remote WMI, and many enterprise tools. Disabling reduces attack surface but may break remote management.
Entries.CautionReason: May break remote management. Disabling DCOM breaks SCCM, remote WMI, and other enterprise remote management tools that depend on DCOM remote activation.
Entries.WhyThisMatters: DCOM remote activation is used by enterprise management tools like SCCM and WMI. Disabling it reduces the remote attack surface but will break workflows that rely on remote COM activation.

Entries.Name: Configure Cipher Suites
Entries.Detail: Sets the preferred SCHANNEL cipher suites to strong AES-256 and AES-128 variants. Ensures strong encryption for all TLS connections.
Entries.WhyThisMatters: Restricts SCHANNEL to AES-256 and AES-128 cipher suites for all TLS connections. Older clients or servers that do not support these suites will fail to negotiate.

Entries.Name: Configure Event Log Sizes
Entries.Description: Increase event log sizes for better auditing
Entries.Detail: Increases the Windows Security event log maximum size to 1 MB. Helps retain more audit events before older entries are overwritten.
Entries.WhyThisMatters: Raises the Windows Security event log cap so audit events are retained longer before they roll over. On active systems with verbose auditing the 1 MB default can fill within hours.

Entries.Name: Configure Key Exchange Algorithms
Entries.Detail: Enables Diffie-Hellman, ECDH, and PKCS key exchange algorithms in SCHANNEL. Ensures forward-secure key exchange is available for TLS connections.
Entries.WhyThisMatters: Enables Diffie-Hellman, ECDH, and PKCS key exchange in SCHANNEL so forward-secure key exchange is available for TLS. Without these, some TLS handshakes fall back to non-forward-secret key exchange.

Entries.Name: Legacy TLS Protocols (1.0/1.1)
Entries.Description: Disable deprecated TLS 1.0 and TLS 1.1 protocols
Entries.Detail: Disables TLS 1.0 and TLS 1.1 (deprecated per RFC 8996, vulnerable to BEAST/POODLE). Also disables PCT 1.0, SSL 2.0, SSL 3.0. TLS 1.2 remains enabled. Use -Enable only for legacy devices that cannot negotiate TLS 1.2+.
Entries.CautionReason: May break VPN or enterprise connectivity. Disabling legacy TLS may break connections to older devices, appliances, or internal services that cannot negotiate TLS 1.2+. Enabling allows known-vulnerable protocols (BEAST/POODLE).
Entries.WhyThisMatters: TLS 1.0 and 1.1 are deprecated per RFC 8996 and are vulnerable to BEAST, POODLE, and related downgrade attacks. Re-enable only if you must interoperate with a device that cannot be upgraded to TLS 1.2.

Entries.Name: Configure Strong .NET Authentication
Entries.Detail: Forces .NET Framework 4.x and 2.x to use system-default TLS and strong cryptography. Prevents older .NET apps from falling back to weak ciphers.
Entries.WhyThisMatters: Forces .NET 4.x and 2.x to negotiate using the OS TLS policy rather than their own hardcoded cipher list. Prevents older .NET apps from silently falling back to weak ciphers when the server offers them.

Entries.Name: Disable AES Ciphers
Entries.Detail: Disables older weak AES cipher entries in the Windows SCHANNEL security layer. Forces applications to negotiate stronger cipher configurations.
Entries.WhyThisMatters: Removes older weak AES cipher entries from the SCHANNEL allowed list so they cannot be negotiated even if a remote peer requests them.

Entries.Name: Disable AutoRun
Entries.Detail: Prevents Windows from automatically running programs on USB drives and other removable media. Stops a common malware spread vector.
Entries.WhyThisMatters: Prevents Windows from executing autorun.inf programs on removable media. This was a primary USB worm propagation mechanism and remains a baseline hardening step even on modern systems.

Entries.Name: Disable IPv6
Entries.Detail: Disables the IPv6 protocol stack system-wide. Recommended if your network does not use IPv6 to reduce unnecessary attack surface.
Entries.WhyThisMatters: Removes the IPv6 stack system-wide. If your network infrastructure does not use IPv6, the protocol expands attack surface without providing value. IPv6-dependent services will break if this is applied to a network that uses it.

Entries.Name: Disable RC2 and RC4 Ciphers
Entries.Detail: Disables RC2 and RC4 ciphers in SCHANNEL. These algorithms are cryptographically broken and should not be used in any TLS connection.
Entries.WhyThisMatters: RC2 and RC4 are cryptographically broken. Any TLS session negotiated with these ciphers can be decrypted offline. No legitimate modern server should require them.

Entries.Name: Disable SMBv3 Compression
Entries.Description: Disable SMBv3 compression to mitigate CVE-2020-0796
Entries.Detail: Turns off SMB compression to protect against CVE-2020-0796. Only affects SMB traffic compression, not file access speed.
Entries.WhyThisMatters: Addresses CVE-2020-0796, a pre-auth remote code execution vulnerability in the SMBv3 compression path. Turning off compression removes the vulnerable code path without affecting normal file transfer throughput.

PlatformSupport.UnavailableReason: SMB compression is supported on Windows 11 and Windows Server 2022 or later.

Entries.Name: Disable TCP Timestamps
Entries.Description: Disable TCP timestamps to reduce network fingerprinting
Entries.Detail: Removes TCP timestamp information from network packets. Prevents remote systems from fingerprinting your OS based on uptime.
Entries.WhyThisMatters: TCP timestamps let remote hosts estimate your system uptime and can narrow OS fingerprinting. Removing them is a passive reconnaissance hardening step.

Entries.Name: Disable Triple DES Cipher
Entries.Detail: Disables Triple DES (3DES) in SCHANNEL. 3DES is vulnerable to SWEET32 birthday attacks and is no longer considered secure.
Entries.WhyThisMatters: 3DES is vulnerable to the SWEET32 birthday attack at practical data volumes. It should not be negotiated in any TLS session.

Entries.Name: Disable Weak Hash Algorithms
Entries.Detail: Configures SCHANNEL hash algorithm settings. Disables MD5 and enables SHA family algorithms for stronger integrity verification.
Entries.WhyThisMatters: Disables MD5 in SCHANNEL and ensures the SHA family is enabled. MD5 is collision-broken and should not appear in certificate or MAC operations.

Entries.Name: Enable Biometrics Anti-Spoofing
Entries.Detail: Enables enhanced anti-spoofing for Windows Hello facial recognition. Only applies if you have compatible biometric hardware.
Entries.WhyThisMatters: Enables enhanced liveness detection for Windows Hello facial recognition on compatible cameras. Without it, a printed photo or screen can potentially unlock the device.

PlatformSupport.UnavailableReason: Windows Hello Enhanced Sign-in Security is a Windows 10/11 client feature.

Entries.Name: Ensure Registry Paths Exist
Entries.Description: Create required registry paths before setting properties
Entries.Detail: Creates any missing registry paths required by other hardening tweaks before they run. This is a prerequisite step with no visible user-facing effect.
Entries.WhyThisMatters: Creates registry key paths that other hardening tweaks expect to exist before they write values. This runs as a prerequisite step and has no direct user-facing effect.

Entries.Name: Filesystem Performance Settings
Entries.Detail: Disables 8.3 short filename generation on NTFS. Improves filesystem performance and removes a legacy compatibility feature rarely needed today.
Entries.WhyThisMatters: Disables 8.3 short filename generation (e.g. PROGRA~1) on NTFS volumes. 8.3 names are a DOS compatibility feature that adds write overhead on every file creation and is rarely needed today.

Entries.Name: General OS Hardening
Entries.Detail: Applies a broad set of registry hardening values covering credential protection, UAC, NTLM, TCP/IP, and smart card removal behavior.
Entries.CautionReason: May break VPN or enterprise connectivity. Changes credential protection, UAC, NTLM, TCP/IP, and smart card policy values. May break legacy authentication, VPN clients, enterprise SSO, or smart-card login workflows.
Entries.WhyThisMatters: Writes a broad set of registry values covering credential guard, UAC, NTLM relay protections, TCP/IP stack hardening, and smart card removal behavior. Review the individual values before applying on domain-joined or managed systems.

Entries.Name: Harden Adobe Reader
Entries.Detail: Applies a set of security policy settings for Adobe Reader DC including protected mode, protected view, and disabling cloud and web features.
Entries.CautionReason: May affect licensing or cloud sync. Restricts Adobe Reader cloud features, automatic updates, and sharing integrations. Documents relying on JavaScript, embedded media, or cloud-based review workflows will be blocked.
Entries.WhyThisMatters: Applies Adobe Reader DC policy settings enabling Protected Mode, Protected View, and Enhanced Security while disabling cloud services, JavaScript execution, and external content loading. May break workflows that depend on Reader's cloud or form features.

Entries.Name: Harden ClickOnce Trust Prompts
Entries.Detail: Disables ClickOnce trust prompts for all security zones. Prevents untrusted ClickOnce applications from prompting for installation.
Entries.CautionReason: May break VPN or enterprise connectivity. Disabling ClickOnce trust prompts prevents installation of ClickOnce-deployed internal enterprise applications, line-of-business tools, and auto-updating utilities.
Entries.WhyThisMatters: Blocks ClickOnce applications from prompting for elevated trust in any security zone. Prevents untrusted .NET applications distributed via ClickOnce from installing without explicit policy approval.

Entries.Name: Harden MS Office
Entries.Detail: Restricts macros, external content execution, and Outlook trust settings across Office versions. Reduces document-based attack vectors.
Entries.CautionReason: May affect licensing or cloud sync. Restricts macros, external content, and Outlook trust settings across Office versions. May break Office automation, VBA-dependent workflows, mail merge, and documents with active content or external data connections.
Entries.WhyThisMatters: Restricts VBA macros to digitally signed sources, blocks external content and DDE links, and tightens Outlook trust settings across Office versions. Document-based attacks frequently rely on these vectors.

Entries.Name: Harden Office Links
Entries.Detail: Prevents Word from automatically refreshing external links in documents and email. Reduces the risk of data exfiltration via embedded document links.
Entries.CautionReason: May affect licensing or cloud sync. Prevents Word from auto-refreshing external links in documents and email. May break mail merge, linked spreadsheets, and templates that pull live data from external sources.
Entries.WhyThisMatters: Stops Word from automatically refreshing external links on document open. Auto-refreshing external links is a data exfiltration technique used in phishing documents to beacon back to an attacker-controlled server.

Entries.Name: Force AMSI Macro Runtime Scanning (Office)
Entries.Description: Force Office macros through AMSI runtime scanning
Entries.Detail: Sets MacroRuntimeScanScope=2 for Word, Excel, PowerPoint, Publisher, Visio, and Access under the Office 16.0 policy hive.
Entries.CautionReason: May break Office macro workflows. Strict AMSI runtime scanning can block or slow VBA automation that relies on dynamic code generation, embedded scripts, or unsigned macro behavior.
Entries.WhyThisMatters: Forces supported Office applications to route macro runtime behavior through AMSI, improving Defender and EDR visibility into macro-borne droppers and script staging.

Entries.Name: Block Legacy RTF Documents in Word
Entries.Description: Block legacy RTF documents in Word
Entries.Detail: Sets Word FileBlock RtfFiles=2 and OpenInProtectedView=0 across Office 14.0, 15.0, and 16.0 policy hives.
Entries.CautionReason: Blocks direct opening of legacy RTF documents in Word. Environments that still exchange RTF templates or mail-merge documents may need to convert them first.
Entries.WhyThisMatters: RTF parser vulnerabilities have repeatedly been used for document-based compromise, including CVE-2023-21716. Blocking RTF in Word removes that parser from routine document-opening paths.

Entries.Name: Restrict OneNote Embedded Files
Entries.Description: Block risky embedded files in OneNote
Entries.Detail: Sets DisableEmbeddedFiles=1 and a blocked-extension list across OneNote 14.0, 15.0, and 16.0 policy hives.
Entries.CautionReason: Breaks OneNote workflows that rely on embedded executables, scripts, archives mounted as disk images, shortcuts, or other high-risk attachments.
Entries.WhyThisMatters: OneNote attachments are a common phishing delivery path after macro blocking became more effective. Blocking high-risk embedded file types reduces click-to-execute payload staging.

Entries.Name: Disable WDigest Credential Caching
Entries.Detail: Sets WDigest UseLogonCredential=0 under HKLM:\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\SecurityProviders\WDigest.
Entries.WhyThisMatters: WDigest credential caching can expose reusable credentials to memory scraping. Forcing UseLogonCredential=0 keeps plaintext WDigest credentials out of LSASS on supported Windows builds.

Entries.Name: Enable Protected Credentials Delegation
Entries.Detail: Sets CredentialsDelegation AllowProtectedCreds=1 under HKLM policy.
Entries.CautionReason: Can affect legacy credential delegation and remoting workflows that do not support protected credential handling.
Entries.WhyThisMatters: Protected credential delegation reduces exposure when credentials cross remoting and delegation boundaries, making credential theft harder after local compromise.

Entries.Name: Enable Auditing Baseline
Entries.Description: Enable command-line process auditing and key auditpol categories
Entries.Detail: Enables ProcessCreationIncludeCmdLine_Enabled and configures auditpol success/failure auditing for process creation, logon, special logon, removable storage, IPsec driver, security state change, system integrity, and sensitive privilege use.
Entries.WhyThisMatters: Command-line process auditing and core security audit categories make incident investigation possible after suspicious execution, privilege use, removable media access, or logon activity.

Entries.Name: Enable PowerShell Transcription
Entries.Description: Enable PowerShell transcription with invocation headers
Entries.Detail: Creates %SystemDrive%\PSTranscripts and enables PowerShell transcription policy with invocation headers.
Entries.CautionReason: Transcripts can contain sensitive command arguments and output. Protect transcript storage with appropriate ACLs and retention policy.
Entries.WhyThisMatters: PowerShell transcription records interactive and scripted command activity, giving defenders a durable trail for suspicious administration and post-exploitation activity.

Entries.Name: Disable Windows PowerShell 2.0 Engine
Entries.Description: Disable the legacy Windows PowerShell 2.0 optional features
Entries.Detail: Disables MicrosoftWindowsPowerShellV2 and MicrosoftWindowsPowerShellV2Root with no automatic restart.
Entries.CautionReason: Breaks legacy scripts or applications that explicitly require the PowerShell 2.0 engine.
Entries.WhyThisMatters: PowerShell 2.0 lacks modern logging and security controls. Removing the legacy engine closes a downgrade path used to avoid script block logging and AMSI.

Entries.Name: CVE-2013-3900 Authenticode Padding Check
Entries.Description: Enable strict Authenticode certificate padding validation
Entries.Detail: Sets EnableCertPaddingCheck=1 in both native and Wow6432Node Wintrust Config policy locations.
Entries.WhyThisMatters: CVE-2013-3900 allows malformed signed files to hide appended content unless stricter Wintrust padding validation is enabled.

Entries.Name: CVE-2021-40444 ActiveX Zone Lockdown
Entries.Description: Block unsafe ActiveX initialization across URL zones
Entries.Detail: Sets URL action 1004=3 across Internet Settings zones 0 through 4.
Entries.CautionReason: Can break legacy intranet sites or enterprise web applications that still depend on ActiveX controls.
Entries.WhyThisMatters: CVE-2021-40444 abused ActiveX initialization through Office and browser-rendered content. Setting action 1004=3 blocks unsafe ActiveX behavior across zones.

Entries.Name: CVE-2022-30190 Remove ms-msdt Handler
Entries.Detail: Deletes Registry::HKEY_CLASSES_ROOT\ms-msdt when present.
Entries.CautionReason: Disables protocol-handler launch of Microsoft Support Diagnostic Tool. Troubleshooting links or legacy support workflows that depend on ms-msdt may stop working.
Entries.WhyThisMatters: The ms-msdt protocol handler was abused by CVE-2022-30190 (Follina) to trigger diagnostic tool execution from crafted documents and URLs.

Entries.Name: Harden WinRM
Entries.Detail: Disables unencrypted WinRM traffic and digest authentication, then restarts the WinRM service. Ensures remote management connections are always encrypted.
Entries.WhyThisMatters: Disables unencrypted WinRM transport and digest authentication, then restarts the WinRM service. After this change, remote PowerShell sessions require HTTPS or Kerberos.

Entries.Name: NetBIOS over TCP/IP Disable
Entries.Description: Disable NetBIOS over TCP/IP on every network adapter
Entries.Detail: Walks HKLM:\System\CurrentControlSet\Services\NetBT\Parameters\Interfaces, snapshots each adapter's NetbiosOptions value into HKLM:\Software\Baseline\NetworkHardening\NetBiosOverTcpip, and writes NetbiosOptions=2 (Disable). Closes the legacy NBT-NS attack surface (poisoning, name-spoofing). Disable restores each adapter's prior NetbiosOptions value from the backup.
Entries.CautionReason: Breaks NetBIOS-only file-share name resolution on legacy LANs. Hosts that rely on NBT broadcast (older SMBv1 file shares, network-attached printers without DNS, legacy domain workgroup browsing) may stop being reachable by short name.
Entries.WhyThisMatters: NetBIOS Name Service (NBT-NS) broadcast resolution is a primary lateral-movement primitive. Tools like Responder rely on NBT-NS poisoning to coerce hashes from clients that fall back to broadcast name resolution after a DNS miss. Setting NetbiosOptions=2 disables NBT entirely and removes that fallback path.

Entries.Name: Network Surface Hardening (TCP/IP, LLMNR, mDNS, RPC)
Entries.Description: Apply the Baseline networking surface-reduction registry catalog
Entries.Detail: Applies a 10-setting catalog: TCP/IP DoS hardening (IGMPLevel=0, DisableIPSourceRouting=2, EnableICMPRedirect=0, TcpMaxDataRetransmissions=3, KeepAliveTime=300000, PerformRouterDiscovery=0, EnableDeadGWDetect=0), LLMNR disable (DNSClient policy EnableMulticast=0), mDNS disable (Dnscache EnableMDNS=0), and RPC endpoint-mapper authentication enforcement (EnableAuthEpResolution=1). Each setting backs its prior value into HKLM:\Software\Baseline\NetworkHardening\<Id> so reversal walks back to the captured original.
Entries.CautionReason: Disabling LLMNR and mDNS breaks zero-config name resolution on flat networks: home LANs, ad-hoc test rigs, AirPrint/Bonjour discovery, and Chromecast/Roku discovery all rely on multicast resolution. RPC endpoint-mapper authentication may break legacy clients that bind to RPC services anonymously.
Entries.WhyThisMatters: LLMNR and mDNS are the second and third name-resolution fallbacks after DNS, both spoofable on the local subnet. The TCP/IP DoS hardening blocks IP source-routing (anti-spoofing) and ICMP redirects (anti-MitM). Together these settings remove a large slice of the local-network attacker toolkit.

Entries.Name: Prevent Remote DLL Hijacking
Entries.Detail: Configures Session Manager DLL search order to prevent applications from loading DLLs from untrusted locations. Closes a common malware delivery path.
Entries.WhyThisMatters: Sets the DLL search order so Windows looks in system directories before the current directory. Applications that bundle DLLs in their working directory and rely on implicit load order may need explicit path updates.

Entries.Name: Prevent Wireless Exploitation
Entries.Detail: Hides the network selection UI from the Windows lock screen. Prevents changing network settings before signing in.
Entries.WhyThisMatters: Removes the network selector from the Windows lock screen so an attacker with physical access cannot change the network connection before authenticating.

Entries.Name: Ransomware Script Extension Lockdown
Entries.Description: Redirect script and remote-launch extensions to Notepad
Entries.Detail: For each ProgID behind .bat .cmd .js .vbs .hta .wsf .reg .msc .rdg .application and .deploy, backs up the current open command under HKLM:\Software\Baseline\RansomwareFtype\<ProgID> and rewrites it to launch Notepad. Double-clicking a payload of these types in Explorer displays the source rather than executing it. Includes the CVE-2020-0765 RDCMan mitigation (.rdg arbitrary resource loading). Disable restores the original commands from the backup.
Entries.CautionReason: Breaks double-click execution of script files. Administrators who routinely double-click .bat/.cmd helper scripts, .reg patches, .msc snap-ins, or .rdg connection files will need to invoke them explicitly (Run as administrator, cmd /c, regedit /s, mmc.exe, or RDCMan.exe). ClickOnce installers (.application/.deploy) will also be neutered.
Entries.WhyThisMatters: Most commodity ransomware and macro-borne droppers stage their initial payload as a script file (.bat/.cmd/.vbs/.js/.hta/.wsf) that the user is tricked into double-clicking. Reassigning these extensions to Notepad converts a single-click execution into a single-click view, neutralising the most common drive-by execution path. Also covers CVE-2020-0765 (RDCMan .rdg parsing).

Entries.Name: Reduce RPC Surface
Entries.Detail: Disables RPC-over-TCP for Task Scheduler and the remote Service Control Manager. Reduces exposure for remote management attack paths.
Entries.CautionReason: May break remote management. Disables RPC-over-TCP for Task Scheduler and Service Control Manager. Breaks remote task scheduling, remote service management, and enterprise tools (SCCM, Intune agents) that depend on those RPC endpoints.
Entries.WhyThisMatters: Disables RPC-over-TCP for the remote Task Scheduler and remote Service Control Manager. These interfaces are used in lateral movement and remote execution techniques.

Entries.Name: WinRM Service Disable
Entries.Description: Stop the WinRM service and set its startup type to Disabled
Entries.Detail: Captures the prior StartType and Status into HKLM:\Software\Baseline\NetworkHardening\WinRMService, stops the WinRM service, and sets its startup to Disabled. Closes the WS-Management remote-management surface that Enter-PSSession, Invoke-Command, and Server Manager depend on. Disable restores the captured prior StartType (and starts the service if it had been Running).
Entries.CautionReason: Breaks remote PowerShell, Enter-PSSession, Invoke-Command, Server Manager remoting, and any agent that binds to WS-Management (some Configuration Manager + Intune workflows). Only enable on hosts that do not need to be managed remotely.
Entries.WhyThisMatters: WinRM exposes a remote command-execution channel often abused for lateral movement (Invoke-Command, PsExec-equivalents). On endpoints that are never managed remotely, stopping and disabling the service eliminates an entire remote-execution attack vector while preserving outbound connectivity.

Entries.Name: Mount Manager NoAutoMount
Entries.WinDefaultDesc: Windows automatically mounts and assigns drive letters to any new volume attached to the system.
Entries.Description: Block automatic mounting of newly attached volumes (USB drives, ISOs, VHDs)
Entries.Detail: Sets HKLM:\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\MountMgr\NoAutoMount=1 so Windows no longer auto-mounts new volumes. Programmatic mounting via mountvol, diskpart, Mount-DiskImage, or Disk Management still works for administrators. Disable removes the value to restore Windows default behaviour.
Entries.CautionReason: Newly attached USB drives, SD cards, and double-clicked ISO/VHD files will not auto-mount. Users must mount them manually (Disk Management or mountvol). Pair with user training in shared environments.
Entries.WhyThisMatters: Auto-mount of unsolicited USB volumes is a known physical-attack delivery path (BadUSB, USB-Rubber-Ducky payloads, malicious LNK/ISO/VHD content). Blocking automatic mounts forces an explicit administrative action before content from untrusted media is exposed to the OS, while preserving programmatic mounting for authorised workflows.

Entries.Name: Browser Enterprise Policies (Edge + Chrome + Firefox + Brave)
Entries.Description: Apply enterprise hardening policies to Microsoft Edge, Google Chrome, Mozilla Firefox, and Brave
Entries.Detail: Writes 32 ADMX-backed policy values across HKLM:\Software\Policies\Microsoft\Edge, HKLM:\Software\Policies\Google\Chrome, HKLM:\Software\Policies\Mozilla\Firefox, and HKLM:\Software\Policies\BraveSoftware\Brave. Edge: SmartScreenEnabled=1, SitePerProcess=1, SSLVersionMin=tls1.2, PasswordManagerEnabled=0, AutofillCreditCardEnabled=0. Chrome: BlockThirdPartyCookies=1, DnsOverHttpsMode=automatic, SafeBrowsingProtectionLevel=2, PasswordManagerEnabled=0, AutofillCreditCardEnabled=0, AutofillAddressEnabled=0. Firefox: DisableTelemetry=1, DisableFirefoxStudies=1, DisableDefaultBrowserAgent=1, SSLVersionMin=tls1.2, PasswordManagerEnabled=0, OfferToSaveLogins=0, OfferToSaveLoginsDefault=0, AutofillCreditCardEnabled=0, AutofillAddressEnabled=0. Brave: Chrome-compatible privacy/password/autofill policies plus Brave rewards, wallet, analytics, stats-ping, web-discovery, and AI-chat disables. Prior values are snapshotted under HKLM:\Software\Baseline\BrowserPolicies so Disable can restore them.
Entries.WhyThisMatters: Browsers are the primary attack surface for drive-by exploitation and credential theft. Enforcing per-site process isolation, modern TLS, enhanced Safe Browsing, telemetry reduction, and disabling in-browser password / credit-card / address autofill reduces exposure to Spectre-class side-channels, downgrade attacks, phishing, and form-grabber malware. Each policy is applied through the browser vendor's documented enterprise policy surface.

Entries.Name: Authentication and Domain Hardening
Entries.Description: Harden Kerberos / LDAP / Netlogon / smart-card / DLL-search policies
Entries.Detail: Writes 9 policy values: Kerberos SupportedEncryptionTypes=0x18 (AES128+AES256 only, disables DES and RC4); LDAPClientIntegrity=2 (require signing); Netlogon RequireSignOrSeal / RequireStrongKey / SealSecureChannel / SignSecureChannel=1; Winlogon ScRemoveOption="1" (lock workstation on smart-card removal); Session Manager SafeDllSearchMode=1 + CWDIllegalInDllSearch=0xFFFFFFFF (block CWD-relative DLL load entirely). Prior values are snapshotted under HKLM:\Software\Baseline\AuthHardening so Disable can restore them. NTLM restrict and PowerShell CLM are deliberately split into a separate Caution toggle.
Entries.WhyThisMatters: Removes legacy weak-crypto primitives (DES, RC4, unsigned LDAP, unsigned Netlogon) that underpin Kerberoasting, AS-REP roasting, NTLM-relay-to-LDAP, and CWD DLL-hijacking attack chains. Smart-card removal lock is a CIS / STIG baseline. The DLL-search hardening is the documented mitigation for binary-planting CVEs that ship year after year.

Entries.Name: NTLM Audit + PowerShell CLM Lockdown
Entries.Description: Audit-then-deny NTLM traffic and force PowerShell Constrained Language Mode
Entries.Detail: Writes 3 policy values: RestrictSendingNTLMTraffic=1 (Audit) and RestrictReceivingNTLMTraffic=1 (Audit) under HKLM:\System\CurrentControlSet\Control\Lsa\MSV1_0 -- audit phase only, intended to surface remaining NTLM consumers in Event Log before stepping up to 2=Deny by hand; PSLockdownPolicy=4 under HKLM:\Software\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\PowerShell -- forces system-wide PowerShell Constrained Language Mode. Prior values are snapshotted under HKLM:\Software\Baseline\AuthHardening so Disable can restore them.
Entries.CautionReason: NTLM restrict at audit level (1) is safe but the next step (2=Deny) on a domain-joined box without first triaging the audit log is the classic foot-gun that locks the machine off the network. PSLockdownPolicy=4 forces every PowerShell session into Constrained Language Mode -- many third-party tools, modules, and DSC configurations break under CLM. Only enable on hosts where a CLM-clean tooling inventory has already been confirmed.
Entries.WhyThisMatters: NTLM is the carrier protocol for relay attacks (PetitPotam, PrintNightmare, NTLM-to-LDAP, NTLM-to-AD-CS). Auditing inbound and outbound NTLM is the documented prerequisite for safely moving the environment to NTLM-deny, which closes those relay chains entirely. PowerShell CLM blocks COM creation, Add-Type, .NET reflection, and module loading from non-allowlisted paths -- the standard offensive PowerShell tradecraft (Invoke-Mimikatz, PowerSploit, Empire) cannot run under CLM.
